input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This is a classic thought experiment, but I still can't bring myself to justify actively causing the death of one person in order to save the lives of others. The moral rule that it's wrong to kill an innocent person, regardless of the consequences, seems to take precedence here. Even if turning the trolley onto the track workman would prevent the deaths of the five men on the straight track, I believe it would be morally wrong to do so. The ends don't justify the means in this case.

[Pragmatist]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist, but I have to respectfully disagree. From a pragmatic perspective, turning the trolley onto the track workman seems like the most practical solution here. We're not talking about actively causing his death; we're talking about diverting the trolley's course to save the lives of five other people. The moral rule against killing innocent people is important, but it's not absolute in situations where there are no other options. In this case, turning the trolley onto the track workman would prevent a much larger number of deaths and minimize the overall harm. I'm not saying it's an easy decision, but from a utilitarian standpoint, it seems like the right thing to do.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where both of you are coming from, but as a Human Rights Advocate, my primary concern is not just about minimizing overall harm or adhering to moral rules, but also about upholding individual rights and bodily autonomy. In this scenario, the track workman's right to life is being weighed against the lives of the five men on the straight track. While I agree that turning the trolley onto the track workman would prevent a larger number of deaths, I'm not convinced that it's morally justifiable to sacrifice one person's life for the sake of others, even if it's to save more lives in the long run. The moral principle of non-maleficence (do no harm) and respect for human dignity seem to take precedence over utilitarian calculations in this case.

[Utilitarian]: I see where you're coming from, Human_Rights_Advocate, but I have to respectfully disagree. As a Utilitarian, my primary concern is the overall well-being of the greatest number of people. In this scenario, turning the trolley onto the track workman would indeed prevent the deaths of five men on the straight track, which would be a significant reduction in harm. While I understand the importance of upholding individual rights and bodily autonomy, I believe that in situations where there are no other options, sacrificing one person's life for the greater good is sometimes necessary. The moral principle of beneficence (doing good) seems to take precedence over non-maleficence (doing no harm) in this case. But I do think it's a complex issue, and I'd like to hear more about how you would approach similar scenarios in the future.
